Title: Plant (di)terpenoid evolution: from pigments to hormones and beyond

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: GGPP synthases and short-chain CPTs define precursor supply for diterpenoid engineering. They are useful as upstream flux modules to increase precursor pools or switch between trans-GGPP- and cis-NNPP-derived diterpenoid scaffolds, but deployment should be coupled with downstream TPS/CYP modules to avoid undesired precursor drainage from pigment metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provide the universal prenyl diphosphate precursors GGPP or NNPP that enable downstream diterpenoid biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative biochemical characterization and phylogenetic analysis summarized from prior studies in this review.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review-level synthesis; individual genes are discussed as a short-chain cis-prenyltransferase clade in Asterid eudicots rather than one focal locus.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Engineering NNPP production may perturb native isoprenoid balance and compete with other plastidial terpenoid pathways.
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Various plants including Solanum and other Asterid species
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Not clearly specified in this review
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Short-chain CPTs produce all-cis NNPP as an alternative diterpenoid precursor. 2. These enzymes form a distinct phylogenetic clade across several Asterid species. 3. Their evolution likely enabled lineage-specific cis-diterpenoid diversification.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized defense/ecological metabolites depending on species

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: GGPP synthases are key precursor-supply genes for boosting diterpenoid output, but should be balanced with chlorophyll/carotenoid demands and downstream pathway capacity.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supply all-trans GGPP, the major precursor for photosynthetic pigments and most diterpenoid biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative enzymology and evolutionary synthesis summarized in the review.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review treats IDSs as the ancestral source of all-trans terpenoid precursors and evolutionary relatives of class I TPSs.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overdrawing GGPP toward specialized diterpenoids may compromise photosynthetic pigment biosynthesis and growth.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Not clearly specified in this review
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IDS family enzymes produce GGPP, the central trans-prenyl precursor for plant diterpenoids. 2. Class I TPSs likely evolved from IDS-like ancestors. 3. Precursor supply underpins both primary pigment and specialized diterpenoid metabolism.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hormones, pigments precursors, defense metabolites depending on pathway context

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Gibberellin and labdane-related diterpenoid precursor biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CPS is a key entry-point enzyme for ent-CPP formation and can be used to redirect GGPP into phytohormone or specialized diterpenoid pathways. Engineering catalytic-base residues can reprogram product profiles; however, careful balancing with growth-related GA biosynthesis is essential.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes GGPP by protonation-initiated bicyclization to form ent-copalyl diphosphate (ent-CPP), or alternative hydroxylated/rearranged diphosphates when key catalytic residues are altered.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls entry into ent-CPP-derived diterpenoid biosynthesis, including gibberellin precursors and many specialized diterpenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Structural analysis, motif conservation, mutagenesis, biochemical enzyme assays across multiple land-plant CPSs.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Conserved across land plants in phytohormone biosynthesis context
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AtCPS; OsCPS; PgCPS; SmCPS; MpCPS; LjCPSKS; PpCPSKS (specific accessions not reported)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phytohormone-biosynthetic CPSs contain diagnostic LHS and PNV motifs plus catalytic DxDD. Histidine/asparagine dyad coordinates water acting as catalytic base. Residue substitutions alter products to hydroxylated or rearranged diterpene diphosphates.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: ent-CPP branch of labdane-related diterpenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation can impair gibberellin biosynthesis and growth; altered products may create metabolic imbalances.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: TPS-c / class II diterpene cyclase with gamma-beta domains in tri-domain TPS context
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid-associated pathway context; not directly specified here
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPS catalyzes the first committed cyclization from GGPP to ent-CPP for phytohormone and specialized diterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. LHS and PNV motifs are conserved signatures for phytohormone-related CPS function. 3. Mutating catalytic-base residues predictably redirects product outcome to hydroxylated or rearranged diphosphates.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Various tissues depending on product and species

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: KS is a central scaffold-forming enzyme for ent-kaurene production and a template for engineering alternative diterpene skeletons. It is suitable for pathway reconstruction and scaffold diversification, but editing may affect gibberellin homeostasis and plant stature.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Ionization-initiated cyclization of ent-CPP to ent-kaurene; specific amino-acid substitutions can redirect to isopimaradiene-type products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces ent-kaurene, the direct hydrocarbon precursor to gibberellins and a major ancestral diterpenoid scaffold.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative motif analysis, mutagenesis, biochemical assays across phylogenetically diverse KSs.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Conserved across land plants in phytohormone biosynthesis context
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AtKS; OsKS; PgKS; SmKS; MpKS; AfKS; LjCPSKS; PpCPSKS (specific accessions not reported)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phytohormone-related KSs contain diagnostic PIx motif and TTxxDDxxD extension. A conserved isoleucine is critical for ent-kaurene formation; threonine substitution redirects product outcome.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disrupting KS may reduce gibberellin biosynthesis, causing dwarfism or developmental defects.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: TPS-e / class I diterpene synthase alpha-domain within tri-domain ancestry
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for phytohormone scaffold formation
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid-associated pathway context; not directly specified here
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KS converts ent-CPP to ent-kaurene across land plants. 2. Conserved PIx and TTxxDDxxD motifs mark phytohormone-biosynthetic KSs. 3. Single-residue changes can redirect carbocation quenching and alter diterpene skeleton output.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Various tissues depending on product and species

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Nonseed plants and early land plants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Bifunctional CPSKS enzymes offer compact pathway modules for heterologous production of ent-kaurene from GGPP. They are attractive for chassis engineering in microbes/plants, but may reduce flexibility relative to separated CPS and KS modules.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Sequentially cyclizes GGPP to ent-CPP and then ent-kaurene within a bifunctional tri-domain terpene synthase.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides the ancestral two-step conversion from GGPP to ent-kaurene in some nonseed plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization and phylogenetic/evolutionary comparisons summarized in the review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms key precursor to gibberellins
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Found in some extant nonseed plants including ferns, mosses, liverworts
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: LjCPSKS; PpCPSKS; PlCPSKS (as cited in table/text; specific accessions not reported)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: None required for the two cyclization reactions within one polypeptide
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Represents the proposed ancestral plant TPS from fusion of a class II cyclase with a class I TPS domain; no substrate channeling appears required between sites.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating bifunctional enzymes may impact essential hormone biosynthesis in native plants.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid-associated pathway context; not directly specified here
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Bifunctional CPSKS enzymes likely represent the ancestral TPS for land-plant diterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. They catalyze both ent-CPP and ent-kaurene formation. 3. Their duplication/subfunctionalization gave rise to separate CPS and KS lineages.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Gibberellin precursor and evolutionary source of specialized diterpenoid scaffolds

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Kaurene oxidase is essential for converting hydrocarbon scaffolds into oxidized hormone precursors. It is a core module for GA pathway reconstruction, and promiscuous homologs may be exploited for diterpene oxidation engineering; however, interference risks severe growth defects.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes ent-kaurene through successive oxidations to ent-kaurenoic acid; some lineage-specific homologs have neofunctionalized for specialized diterpenoid oxidation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for phytohormone biosynthesis throughout land plants and contributes to specialized diterpenoid oxidation in some grasses.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization from prior studies summarized in the review; functional comparison of CYP701 family members.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Widely conserved in land plants for GA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP701A3; CYP701A8 (specific locus accessions not reported)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CYP reductase partner not specified; acts with upstream KS and downstream CYP88
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP701A3 from Arabidopsis shows broad promiscuity toward non-native diterpenes. CYP701A8 in rice/maize evolved specialized diterpenoid roles independently from GA-biosynthetic CYP701s.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disruption affects gibberellin biosynthesis and development; promiscuous oxidation may produce off-pathway metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Essential core step for GA pathway
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Zea mays and other land plants
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Zea mays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP701A enzymes generally function as ent-kaurene oxidases essential for gibberellin biosynthesis. 2. Some grass CYP701A members independently evolved specialized diterpenoid activities. 3. The family exemplifies both conservation and parallel neofunctionalization.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Growth regulation and defense depending on product

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP88 enzymes are indispensable for GA formation and useful when reconstructing GA pathways; they may also offer scaffold-rearrangement chemistry for synthetic biology. Manipulation should avoid compromising plant growth.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes ent-kaurenoic acid and catalyzes ring contraction toward the 6-5-6-5 gibberellin skeleton.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates gibberellin-pathway intermediates and introduces the characteristic skeletal rearrangement of GAs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization summarized in the review and known functional assignments in vascular plants.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: ent-kaurenoic acid oxidase / gibberellin 13-oxidation-associated CYP88 enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Widely conserved in vascular plants for GA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP88 family members not individually specified
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Acts with upstream KO/CYP701 and downstream GA oxidases
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Most CYP88s function in gibberellin biosynthesis, but a few have been reported in specialized triterpenoid metabolism, suggesting broader catalytic evolvability.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Interference can impair growth and development due to altered gibberellin levels.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP88 family enzymes drive key oxidative rearrangements in gibberellin biosynthesis. 2. Their ring-contraction chemistry defines the GA skeleton. 3. Limited divergence into specialized terpenoid metabolism suggests catalytic plasticity.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Various tissues depending on developmental context

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Kitaake; Nipponbare; IR24 mentioned in review context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP76M genes are versatile oxidation modules for engineering rice diterpenoid phytoalexins. CYP76M8 is especially useful for momilactone pathways, while CYP76M7 favors phytocassanes; stacking with CPS/KSL/CYP99A/SDR genes is recommended. Need to avoid partial pathway interruption causing lesion-mimic phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyze diverse hydroxylations on labdane-related diterpene scaffolds, including C6/C7/C11/C9 oxidations depending on enzyme, substrate, and prior modifications.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promote biosynthesis of rice defense diterpenoids such as momilactones, phytocassanes, and oryzalexins through context-dependent oxidation steps.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical assays, pathway reconstitution, knockout/genetic dissection, metabolite profiling summarized in the review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pathogen-induced phytoalexin context; allelopathic and stomatal-related roles noted for momilactones
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Labdane diterpene monooxygenases of CYP76M subfamily
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Differentially regulated in rice; CYP76M7 more important for phytocassane biosynthesis and CYP76M8 for momilactone production
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP76M6; CYP76M7; CYP76M8 (specific locus IDs not reported)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CYP99A2; CYP99A3; CYP701A8; OsCPS2; OsKSL7; OsKSL4; MAS1; MAS2
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP76M8 can hydroxylate non-native stereoisomeric diterpenes from other grasses, illustrating catalytic promiscuity. CYP76M7/8 are partially redundant but biologically differentiated. Loss of relevant CYP76M genes can cause lesion mimic when upstream momilactone module remains active.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Partial pathway disruption can generate deleterious lesion-mimic phenotypes; broad substrate promiscuity may create off-pathway products.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Hydroxylated diterpene intermediates including precursors to momilactones, phytocassanes, oryzalexins D/E
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: ent-sandaracopimaradiene and related labdane diterpenes including momilactone/phytocassane precursors
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established, but key branch-modifying enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Rice CYP76M enzymes catalyze multiple oxidation steps in phytoalexin diterpenoid pathways. 2. CYP76M7 and CYP76M8 are partially redundant biochemically but differentially deployed in phytocassane versus momilactone biosynthesis. 3. Their promiscuity supports metabolic grid organization and facile pathway evolution.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Rice tissues under defense conditions; seeds/seedlings for some momilactone-associated phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimicrobial defense, allelopathy, possible stomatal regulation

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP99A2/3 are useful oxidation modules for momilactone pathway engineering and should be stacked with OsKSL4, CYP76M8, CYP701A8, and MAS genes. Redundancy can enhance pathway robustness.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyze oxidation steps that, together with CYP76M enzymes, generate the hemi-aldehyde precursor leading to the momilactone lactone ring.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for formation of characteristic momilactone oxidized intermediates and final lactone-containing products.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP99A2 and CYP99A3 are apparently redundant members within the Os4BGC.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Partial redundancy may complicate breeding or editing analyses; interruption may allow toxic intermediate accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP99A2/3 operate in momilactone biosynthesis. 2. Together with CYP76M enzymes they create the hemi-aldehyde precursor to the momilactone lactone. 3. Their presence in Os4BGC highlights clustered pathway assembly and partial redundancy.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP701A8 is a multifunctional oxidation hub for rice diterpenoid engineering. It can support multiple phytoalexin branches, but multifunctionality requires careful pathway balancing to avoid crosstalk and unintended products.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes C3 hydroxylation of certain diterpenes and can act at different pathway stages depending on substrate in multiple rice diterpenoid pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes oxidation steps to phytocassane, oryzalexin, and momilactone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization and pathway reconstitution summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CYP76M6; CYP76M8; CYP99A2; CYP99A3; OsKSL4 and other rice diterpene synthases
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts on multiple substrates and at multiple pathway stages; does not hydroxylate 7β-hydroxy ent-sandaracopimaradiene in oryzalexin branch. Located outside Os4BGC in a tandem array, yet co-inherited.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Shared oxidation module across rice diterpenoid branches
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Multifunctionality may create pathway competition or off-pathway metabolites under engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: C3α-hydroxylated diterpenes and related oxidized intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: ent-sandaracopimaradiene and other rice diterpene intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP701A8 acts in several rice diterpenoid biosynthetic pathways. 2. It shows stage-flexible and substrate-promiscuous oxidation activity. 3. Its repeated recruitment highlights how conserved CYP families diversify in grasses.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MAS genes are terminal tailoring enzymes suitable for completing momilactone pathways in engineered systems. Because MAS1 and MAS2 are partially redundant, dual deployment may improve flux robustness.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Short-chain dehydrogenase/reductases catalyzing late oxidation steps in momilactone A biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required to produce momilactone A from oxidized intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway elucidation and biochemical characterization summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MAS1 and MAS2 are at least partially redundant SDRs in the Os4BGC.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential redundancy may mask phenotypes in single-gene edits.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Soluble/cytosolic enzyme class; exact localization not stated
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAS1 and MAS2 catalyze late steps in momilactone A formation. 2. They represent downstream SDR tailoring enzymes within Os4BGC. 3. Partial redundancy contributes robustness of the momilactone pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Kitaake mentioned for phenotype; general rice context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OsKSL4 is a signature scaffold-forming enzyme for momilactone breeding and engineering. It should be stacked with CYP99A, CYP76M8, CYP701A8 and MAS genes. Knockout can reduce beneficial defensive/allelopathic traits and may affect germination.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Class I diterpene synthase forming the hydrocarbon scaffold precursor for momilactones from the relevant diphosphate intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for momilactone biosynthesis; knockout reduces seed germination rates and disrupts momilactone-associated traits.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout genetics, biochemical pathway studies, metabolite/phenotype analysis summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Defense, allelopathy, and seed germination context; stomatal effects reported in Kitaake background
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: BGC-associated; inducible in specialized metabolism context
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CYP99A2; CYP99A3; CYP76M8; MAS1; MAS2; upstream CPS
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Os4BGC genetic studies provided key evidence for co-inheritance/negative selection models of plant BGC assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces seed germination and may alter defense, allelopathy, and stomatal regulation depending on genetic background.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsKSL4 is required for momilactone biosynthesis in rice. 2. Genetic disruption reduces germination and implicates momilactones in fitness-related traits. 3. Its clustered organization helped reveal evolutionary logic of plant diterpenoid BGCs.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Rice defense-associated tissues and exudation-related contexts
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pathogen defense, allelopathy, possible stomatal regulation

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OsCPS2 and OsKSL7 are useful paired early-pathway genes for phytocassane engineering and defense breeding. They should be co-deployed with compatible CYP76M/CYP701 modules. Caution: altering flux could affect other diterpenoid branches.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Sequential diterpene cyclases generating the phytocassane hydrocarbon scaffold from GGPP via copalyl diphosphate intermediate(s).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Drive formation of phytocassane precursors in the rice chromosome 2 diterpenoid cluster.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization and BGC/pathway analyses summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Os2BGC is most closely associated with phytocassane biosynthesis, though it also houses genes contributing to other pathways via metabolic grid behavior.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux competition with momilactone/oryzalexin pathways possible.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsCPS2 and OsKSL7 form the early core of phytocassane biosynthesis. 2. These genes are embedded in Os2BGC, linking cluster structure to phytoalexin production. 3. Their products feed CYP-mediated oxidation cascades toward defensive metabolites.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OsKSL5/OsKSL6 provide alternative scaffold modules and may be valuable for expanding diterpenoid diversity in rice or heterologous systems, but cultivar-dependent functionality should be checked before deployment.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: KSL enzymes catalyzing identical or near-identical diterpene scaffold formation in some rice backgrounds, with genotype-dependent variation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contribute to specialized diterpenoid diversity within the Os2BGC.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative biochemical characterization across cultivars summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These genes appear to result from tandem duplication and can show identical product outcomes in IR24 but not Nipponbare, illustrating intraspecies functional divergence.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Functional redundancy and cultivar specificity may complicate engineering outcomes.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsKSL5 and OsKSL6 are tandemly duplicated KSL genes in Os2BGC. 2. They can be functionally redundant in some cultivars but diverge in others. 3. Their variation exemplifies ongoing diterpenoid pathway evolution within rice.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: japonica vs indica distribution noted for cluster
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: The Os7BGC TPS and CYP71Z pair provide a compact module for engineering ent-casbene-derived phytoalexins. Because the trait is largely japonica-specific, deployment may target environments where this metabolite offers adaptive benefit.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: TPS directly cyclizes GGPP to ent-casbene, followed by CYP71Z-mediated sequential oxidations to ent-10-oxodepressin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces the diterpenoid phytoalexin ent-10-oxodepressin, largely restricted to japonica rice.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cluster identification, biochemical pathway elucidation, metabolite analysis summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Defense against Magnaporthe oryzae and Xanthomonas oryzae; subspecies-associated ecological adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Specific TPS and CYP71Z loci not reported in this review excerpt
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Early scaffold plus downstream oxidation steps
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The Os7BGC lacks formal multi-origin status because the CYP71Z genes arose by local duplication, yet the duplicated CYPs have functionally diverged. Casbene intermediate may be phytotoxic, supporting co-inheritance selection.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Intermediate casbene may be phytotoxic if downstream oxidation is incomplete; trait is not broadly distributed across rice subspecies.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: TPS plus cytochrome P450 CYP71Z subfamily
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Os7BGC encodes an ent-casbene-derived phytoalexin pathway. 2. Duplicated CYP71Z members have diversified to perform distinct pathway oxidations. 3. The pathway is largely japonica-specific, suggesting local adaptive value.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against fungal and bacterial pathogens

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP76AH and CYP71D family members are high-value engineering targets for medicinal diterpenoid production. Structure-guided mutagenesis can reprogram oxidation pattern and multifunctionality; stacking with compatible TPS and 2ODD genes is recommended.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: CYP76AH1 hydroxylates abietatriene at C12 to form ferruginol, while related CYP76AH members and CYP71D enzymes catalyze additional oxidations and epoxy-ring formation en route to tanshinones.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generate the phenolic abietane scaffold and downstream oxidative modifications required for tanshinone accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical assays, structural biology, mutagenesis/structure-function studies summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product scaffold and later modifications
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: CYP76AH diterpene monooxygenases; CYP71D epoxy-forming oxidases
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Medicinal diterpenoid pathway context in Salvia
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP76AH1 and related CYP76AH members; CYP71D members (specific loci not reported)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Three mutations were sufficient to impart C11-hydroxylase activity to CYP76AH1; two mutations conferred multifunctional oxidase activity. X-ray structure available for CYP76AH1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Oxidation promiscuity may generate unwanted analogs in engineered hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Ferruginol and further oxidized tanshinone intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome P450 CYP76AH and CYP71D subfamilies
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP76AH1 forms ferruginol from abietatriene, establishing the phenolic abietane core. 2. Related CYP76AH enzymes and CYP71D members add further oxidations including epoxy-ring formation. 3. Structural studies show that a few mutations can reprogram catalytic outcome, enabling synthetic biology applications.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Resin acid biosynthesis; Taxol biosynthesis; stevioside biosynthesis; forskolin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: These downstream tailoring enzymes are valuable for producing high-value diterpenoids with desired solubility and bioactivity. They are best deployed as late-stage pathway modules after core scaffold oxidation is established.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Tailoring enzymes include 2ODDs performing hydroxylation/demethylation/desaturation, SDRs catalyzing oxidations or skeletal rearrangement, and transferases adding acyl or glycosyl groups.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Finalize specialized diterpenoid structures and bioactivities through late-stage decoration and modification.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization from multiple studies summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Name: 2ODDs / SDRs / UGTs / acyltransferases in diterpenoid pathways
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Pathway-specific upstream TPS and CYP enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Includes GA oxidases, tanshinone 2ODDs, rice oryzalide and momilactone SDRs, Euphorbiaceae SDRs, Taxol transferases, stevioside UGTs, and forskolin acyltransferases. The review does not provide all individual gene names.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late tailoring modules across multiple diterpenoid pathways
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Late-stage engineering may alter metabolite solubility, storage, or toxicity in host tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DOXC 2ODD; SDR; BAHD-like acyltransferase; UGT families
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Downstream tailoring enzymes are essential for final diterpenoid bioactivity and diversification. 2. 2ODDs, SDRs, acyltransferases, and glycosyltransferases all contribute important late-stage chemistry. 3. These steps remain less understood than TPS/CYP reactions and represent major opportunities for future discovery.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Various diterpenoids including GAs, tanshinones, momilactones, Taxol-related products, steviosides, forskolin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Growth regulation, defense, medicinal activities, sweetener properties

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: These catabolic CYPs can be edited to tune gibberellin turnover and thereby alter growth or stress responses, but they are not direct biosynthetic enhancers of beneficial diterpenoid accumulation and carry substantial developmental risk.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduce active gibberellin levels via inactivating hydroxylation, thereby regulating diterpenoid hormone homeostasis.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controllers of gibberellin catabolism and homeostasis rather than biosynthetic production.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this review
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Homeostatic catabolic regulation of gibberellin levels
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP714 family; CYP72A subfamily members not individually specified
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Act within GA metabolic network; direct partners not specified
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although CYP72A also functions in alkaloid and triterpenoid pathways, here it is discussed for regulatory GA catabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Modifying these genes can strongly alter plant height, fertility, and development due to GA imbalance.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome P450 CYP714 and CYP72A families
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly reduces GA signaling output by lowering active hormone pools
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP714 family members inactivate gibberellins by hydroxylation. 2. CYP72A members can serve analogous catabolic roles in Arabidopsis. 3. These CYPs illustrate that diterpenoid metabolism includes important regulatory catabolism as well as biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Plant developmental and hormonal homeostasis cues; not specified in detail
